commentary: First wood-firing of the season at the Higashiyama Craft Collective. Three days of stoking and the ash glaze came out richer than I dared hope.
Testing a new pine-ash glaze recipe this week. The way Kyoto humidity shifts the melt never stops surprising me.
Honoured to share that my commission for the Kyoto Craft Museum permanent collection is now under way. Years of wheel-throwing leading to this.
A quiet morning at the wheel before the studio fills up. Hand-building a series of tea bowls for the Shibui Gallery show.
Kiln behaviour notes: dropped the reduction window by twenty minutes and the iron speckle finally read the way I wanted. Small changes, big results.
Packing work for Galerie Terre in Paris. There is something humbling about Kyoto clay travelling across the world.
Studio update: new batch of local clay from Kyoto Clay Supply. Wedging it in feels like meeting an old friend with a slightly different temperament.
Spent the afternoon talking glaze chemistry with Toshio Saito in Shigaraki. Forty years of experience in every sentence.
Opening night at the Shibui Gallery. Grateful to everyone who came out to see the wood-ash series in person.
Cold mornings mean slower drying. Wrapping the larger vessels in plastic to keep the rims from cracking before the bisque.
End-of-year reflection: this was the year I trusted the kiln to do its own thing. Less control, more dialogue with the fire.
New year, new test tiles. Forty glaze combinations lined up for the first firing of 2026. Documentation is half the craft.
Wheel-throwing a set of matched yunomi today. Consistency across a series is its own quiet discipline.
Visiting potter Park Jisoo stopped by the collective from Seoul. Comparing Korean and Japanese approaches to celadon was a joy.
Glaze chemistry deep dive: balancing silica and alumina for a stable matte at cone 10. Sharing my notebook page for anyone curious.
Spring light in the Higashiyama studio makes the celadon glow. Photographing the new bowls with Riku Hasegawa this week.
Delivered the first finished pieces for the Kyoto Craft Museum commission today. A long bow to the curators for their trust.
Talking contemporary Japanese ceramics with students through Naoko Ogawa's arts programme. Passing on what was passed to me.
Resetting the studio after the museum delivery. Twenty-five square metres of calm before the next body of work begins.
Summer firing planning underway. Stacking the kiln is a three-dimensional puzzle I will happily spend a whole day solving.
